# Program: Lifestyle changes and life expectancy

Program: Health Report

Sun 3 May 2009 at 10:39pm Sunday 3 May 2009 at 10:39pm Sun 3 May 2009 at 10:39pm

Space to play or pause, M to mute, left and right arrows to seek, up and down arrows for volume. Play

Duration: 12 minutes 25 seconds 12 m

Brought to you by

[ABC Radio National](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/radionational/)

Presented by

[Dr Norman Swan](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/radionational/norman-swan/2921040)

New research from the University of Cambridge in the UK shows the benefits of simple lifestyle changes on measurable increases in life expectancy.

## Credits

* [Norman Swan, Presenter](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/radionational/norman-swan/2921040)
* [Brigitte Seega, Producer](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/radionational/brigitte-seega/2915420)

Program: More from Health Report

Diet and Nutrition, Cancer, Heart Disease, Exercise and Fitness

## Transcript

**Norman Swan:** We're beaten about the head all the time to change our lifestyle with the promise that it'll make us live longer and in better health. But what should we change and will it just mean that we live longer miserably rather than well?

A large study in the UK suggests you can boil the answer down to just four factors and the news is good. Kay-Tee Khaw is Professor of Clinical Gerontology at the University of Cambridge.

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** This prospective study is a cohort of 25,000 men and women who were aged 40 to 79 years when we first started in 1993 or so. It was initially started as part of a ten country European collaboration to try and identify dietary factors that might modify the risk of cancer and the reason for the European collaboration was because across Europe there are huge variations in cancer risk as well as dietary patterns.

But when we were doing this particular study, which is based in Norfolk in the United Kingdom, we thought this was a great opportunity to follow up health as a whole in this large general community of men and women, to try and identify not just what dietary factors might influence cancer but to broaden the approach to find out what we can do to maintain health as we get older.

**Norman Swan:** There have been many of these prospective studies throughout the world, the most famous being Framingham in Massachusetts. So how did you follow these people through, what did you measure and how intensively were they followed, are they being followed?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Well we're very fortunate in Britain in that it's much easier to follow people, at least for mortality, because everybody can be flagged in the National Statistics Office. So we know everyone's cause of death and whether they're dead or alive. But what we also have in the United Kingdom is a very good cancer registry, so we can have a very good documentation of everyone who's developed cancer, the type of cancer, the staging of cancer -- but even more interesting is that because of the health service we have total record linkage with all hospitalisations. So everybody in our cohort who has been to hospital we can document what the reasons were.

**Norman Swan:** And just one examination at the beginning where you find out what they eat, how much exercise they get, or do you take into account that these things might change and you look at it ten years later?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Yes, there were two things we think that may have helped our identification of behaviours. The first is that we measure these serially over time and we also bring them back from time to time, though of course this is much more expensive. But what is really difficult to measure is usual diet, usual physical activity.

The food frequency questionnaire is the standard instrument that's been used but people have to rely on their memory and judgment to assess what they eat regularly. So we used the 7 day diet, where people carry round a notebook with them and they write down every single thing they eat. So it's rooted in reality.

Similarly with physical activity that people in the past have relied on various self reports on physical activity, we've developed a questionnaire that was based on objective validation using metabolic rate, heart rate, and this seems again to relate better to health outcomes.

We also have a lot of objective markers of diet which were not possible in the past. New technologies have enabled us to measure, say, things like fatty acids, similarly with salt and sodium potassium we measure that in urine, blood markers of different carotinoids. We think that perhaps some of the controversy about diet and health may be because in the past we've not been able to assess it very precisely.

**Norman Swan:** So you've just looked at some lifestyle factors and how long you live and you've boiled it down to four variables. Tell us the finding.

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** The four variables we chose were because these are the ones that are usually recommended. I think what is less clear is that we don't know whether because they are highly correlated one's just a mark of another. For example, people who smoke or don't smoke are more or less likely to be physically active, have different dietary patterns, for example, so we wanted to see whether each of these lifestyle factors independently had an effect on health.

The other big criticism about lifestyle modification and health is that 'well, within the range that it's possible to change in everyday life is not going to have any measurable impact on my health, so unless I start running marathons or, you know, jump up and down for, what, three hours every day, I'm not going to be able to have an influence on my health'. So we were particularly interested to find out in this middle-aged, free-living population in the United Kingdom whether across a range of usual behaviours that you find that people choose in everyday life, whether there was a measurable relationship with mortality, with survival.

And we found that four health behaviours: not smoking, moderate alcohol intake, fruit and vegetables (five servings a day), and moderate physical activity, that is, not being an absolute couch potato -- these are very, very moderate health behaviours. But if you score one for each of these health behaviours, people who scored four, compared to zero, had a survival comparable to someone who was 14 years younger in age. So this appeared to be quite a big measurable impact, a fourfold difference in mortality associated with these four relatively small and modest lifestyle differences.

**Norman Swan:** So wasn't out to the extreme...and what about the controversy about the moderate alcohol use, given that there are now several people who are suggesting that there's no relationship here between alcohol and mortality and longevity.

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** There's a lot of controversy about the relationship between alcohol and longevity, mainly because alcohol has different relationships to different end points. For example I think that the relationship with heart disease, cardiovascular disease, is generally well accepted that there's a U-shaped relationship.

**Norman Swan:** U-shaped meaning with moderate intake you get a dip down?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Very moderate amounts, one to two drinks a day there is some protection but of course at very high levels there's increased risk again. What we define as moderate alcohol intake was more than zero, one to fourteen units a week was on average two drinks a day. And that was the amount of alcohol that was associated with good mortality. I should stress though that this was in middle aged populations where the biggest killers are heart disease, and this might not necessarily apply to a much younger group. For example in young men who don't get heart disease but the main cause of death is violent death from accidents, and then we might not see the same relationship between alcohol and mortality. But certainly in late middle life where heart disease is the major killer we see this relationship with alcohol. So the advice has to be very specific to age groups.

**Norman Swan:** And what does it actually mean in terms of these four variables? Does it mean that if at age 40 you have these four factors you're more likely to live fourteen years longer? Or if at any point you have them it's going to have an impact? In other words there must be a dose effect, surely, of time that you have these factors and how long you are going to live.

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** What we can't tell is whether these lifestyle factors are cumulative throughout life or just a reflection of a particular point in time. What we do know is that from other studies changing lifestyles does appear to have an impact on health. For example we know that giving up smoking has an almost immediate impact on your risk of heart disease. You can see that in many, many studies. There's evidence that changing behaviours even late in life seems to have a measurable impact. We see this also in the trials of blood pressure treatment, for example, that you can treat people who have high cholesterol or high blood pressure, even though they've had lifelong high levels of cholesterol and blood pressure. But even if you treat them in their 60s and 70s there is still a reduction subsequently in heart disease and mortality. So we know that changing risk factors late in life still has a big impact on subsequent health.

**Norman Swan:** So the implication then is...just give us the four variables again.

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Not smoking, not being totally physically inactive -- that is, even having a standing occupation or half an hour of jogging or swimming a day, physical activity -- moderate alcohol, that is, more than one and less than 14 units a week, on average 2 drinks a day, and fruit and vegetables.

We measured blood vitamin C as an indicator for fruit and vegetable intake, on average five servings of fruit and vegetables a day, which some people say is a lot, but actually it isn't if you count a small serving as a tangerine, or an apple; two servings of vegetables at lunch or dinner.

**Norman Swan:** Although you can't say that by doing that you're actually going to change it, it's an observational study, the implication is that if you spend your middle age with those kind of variables you can expect to live a few years longer?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Yes, well all we can say is what the data showed, and the data showed that the survival of people with these health behaviours had a survival comparable to somebody who was 14 years older.

**Norman Swan:** And if you look at other studies, the Japanese have done a study called Exceptional Survivors where again the same variables predict men who get to the age of 85 in pretty good nick, but they added some work which is called psychosocial variables, psychological social variables, one in particular, being married, helped to predict it. Did any of these psychological or social variables matter to your model?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Yes, though not in this particular model, my colleague who has measured psychosocial factors in this population has reported that psychosocial factors -- for example a high sense of coherence.

**Norman Swan:** Coherence?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Yes, three questions on whether you find life meaningful, manageable, that a high sense of coherence is associated with a 15% lower mortality risk independent of other lifestyle factors. And I should say also that the other lifestyle factors I've been talking about were independent of age and social class and obesity, so a lot of these factors independently seem to contribute a small amount. Each on its own may not seem a lot, one or two years, but if you add them up collectively they seem to have a major impact.

**Norman Swan:** And what did the coherence translate to in terms of years?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** That's a few years, that's about three or four years difference.

**Norman Swan:** And what have you found if anything about the original name of the study, which was diet and cancer?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Cancer takes a longer time to report on because it's a less common end point, so we've had to combine results with the whole of the European study to document some of the cancer findings. But some interesting findings for example are that everyone will be aware that there's a lot of controversy about meat intake and colorectal cancer risk; there's a lot of controversy. We've also reported in EPIC that fibre is protective for colorectal cancer, again there's a bit of controversy about that. But what we were able to do in Europe, because we had far more cases that we could analyse and there was a much bigger range of dietary variation, was that there was a strong interaction between meat and fibre intake -- in that the people who were eating very high fibre intakes, meat did not predict colorectal cancer, whereas people who were eating low fibre intakes, meat was predictive of colorectal cancer.

**Norman Swan:** There might be an interaction between the two.

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** There might be an interaction between the two.

**Norman Swan:** Which could explain the trials which have failed to show a benefit of fibre.

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** Yes. It may well be that if you have a high meat intake and a very high fibre intake then it doesn't seem to be associated with an adverse colorectal cancer risk. And I think that maybe some of the premature conclusions we've jumped to from previous studies have been too simplistic, we haven't looked at the whole dietary pattern, looked at dietary interactions and that we need to look much more at the whole dietary pattern when we are making dietary recommendations.

One more thing I would like to add is that most of our studies now have focused very much on longevity and mortality, but in ageing populations what people are most interested in is not actually just to have a long life but to have a high quality life. So what we're really focusing a lot of effort on in EPIC now is functional health, measuring objective functional health, quality of life. And what's very encouraging is that we've also looked at physical performance, mental functional health, quality of life, and that these lifestyles seem to relate also to functional health or quality of life measures.

**Norman Swan:** So the causes of a longer life are also the causes of a longer life free of disability or relatively free of disability?

**Kay-Tee Khaw:** It seems to be the case.

**Norman Swan:** Kay-Tee Khaw is Professor of Clinical Gerontology at the University of Cambridge.

References:

Khaw KT et al. Combined Impact of Health Behaviours and Mortality in Men and Women: The EPIC-Norfolk Prospective Population Study. _PLoS Med_ 5(1):e12.doi:10.1371/journal.pmed.0050012

Khaw KT et al. Correction: Combined Impact of Health Behaviours and Mortality in Men and Women: The EPIC-Norfolk Prospective Population Study. _PLoS Med_ 5(1):e12.doi:10.1371/journal.pmed.0050012
